title: Day 22 — Machine Coding Interview
summary: Interview Week begins: 15-minute revision sprint, 90-minute Kanban Board machine coding, six debugging fixes, structured code review, and 20 interview questions — no new theory.

blocks.content: Week 4 is Interview Week. You have built weather dashboards, movie search, shopping carts, theme systems, Redux todos, RTK Query products, auth flows, and admin dashboards. Today you prove you can do it cold — under a timer, with an interviewer watching, explaining every decision. No new React concepts. Only review, build, debug, review code, and answer questions like your offer depends on it.

blocks.title: Interview rules
blocks.content: Set a 90-minute timer for machine coding. Narrate your thinking out loud. When stuck, state what you know and what you would look up in official docs. Do not introduce concepts from outside Weeks 1–3. Today is practice, not learning.

rows: map/filter/reduce return new arrays — never mutate in Redux or React state
Filter tasks per column; map to TaskCard props
Spread copies shallowly; nested updates need nested spread
Inner functions remember outer variables — hooks and debounce depend on this
Debounce search filter; watch stale closures in effects
async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises; always try/catch fetch
fetchTasks on mount; handle 404 and network errors
Microtasks before macrotasks; explains Promise.then vs setTimeout order
setTimeout debounce; understand why state updates batch
Wait for pause before firing — reduces API calls on search
Optional bonus: filter tasks by title with debounced input

blocks.title: If you blank on closures
blocks.content: Say: a function that remembers the environment where it was created. Example: debounce timer id lives in closure. Move on — do not ramble.

blocks.title: Before you code, confirm you can explain each

items.text: Components — functional, single responsibility, composition over inheritance
Props — data down, callbacks up, never mutate props
State — useState for local, lift when siblings need same data
useEffect — external sync only; correct deps; cleanup for subscriptions
Context — global low-frequency state; memoize value; custom hooks
Router — Route, Link, useParams, Navigate, protected routes
Redux — slice, actions, selectors, immutable updates via RTK
RTK Query — useGetTasksQuery, cache tags, mutations with invalidation
Performance — profile first; memo, useMemo, useCallback where measured

blocks.content: Interviewers expect typed props and API models even under time pressure. Spend two minutes defining types before JSX.

blocks.title: Props typing rule
blocks.content: Every component gets an interface. No any. If API shape differs from UI shape, map explicitly — do not cast blindly.

Your assigned project today (randomly selected from the interview pool): Kanban Board. Time limit: 90 minutes. Requirements: responsive, TypeScript, API integration, clean architecture, loading, error, and empty states.

blocks.title: Interviewer script — read aloud before starting
blocks.content: Build a Kanban board with three columns: To Do, In Progress, Done. Fetch initial tasks from an API. User can add tasks, move between columns, and delete. Handle loading, error, and empty states. TypeScript required. You have 90 minutes. Talk me through your approach.

items.text: 0–10 min: Clarify scope, define types, scaffold folders, static column layout
10–30 min: Local state — add, move, delete tasks with immutable updates
30–50 min: API fetch, loading skeleton, error + retry, empty states
50–70 min: Responsive polish, shared TaskCard component, keyboard basics
70–90 min: Self-test flows, fix bugs, prepare 60-second architecture explanation

blocks.content: After machine coding, open the provided buggy Kanban starter (or audit your own submission). Find and fix each bug. Document root cause in one sentence per bug — interviewers ask why, not just what you changed.
Symptom: app freezes. Look for useEffect without deps that sets state, or setState directly in component body. Fix: dependency array, guard clause, or move logic to event handler.
Symptom: UI stale after move. Look for .push(), direct array index assignment, or mutating object inside state. Fix: map/filter/spread returning new references.
Symptom: stale data when filter changes. Look for empty [] when effect uses props or state. Fix: add deps or extract stable callback with useCallback.
Symptom: wrong task deleted. Look for key={index}. Fix: key={task.id} with stable unique ids.
Symptom: crash on 500. Look for missing try/catch and unchecked response.ok. Fix: error state + retry button.
Symptom: task detail page blank. Look for path mismatch between Route and Link, untyped useParams, missing NotFound. Fix: align paths, guard undefined params.

blocks.text: Code Review — Review Your Own Submission

blocks.content: Pretend you are the senior engineer reviewing a junior's PR. Score yourself honestly on each axis. Write one improvement per axis — this is exactly Round 4 material on Day 24.

rows: Can I find api, types, components in under 10 seconds?
Do TaskCard, useTasks, fetchTasks read clearly?
Can I tab through add, move, delete?
div onClick with no keyboard support
Is TaskCard shared across three columns?

blocks.title: Code review output required
blocks.content: Write 5 bullet points: one strength, four improvements. Bring this to Day 23 production refactor.

Block 30–60 minutes. Answer all 20 questions out loud. Record yourself if possible — ums, pauses, and circular answers are fixable only if you hear them. Categories: JavaScript, React, TypeScript, Architecture, Performance.
I don't need the perfect answer. I need to see how you think when you don't know.

items.text: Kanban Board built in 90-minute time box
Six debugging bugs fixed with documented root cause
Self code review written — 5 bullets
20 interview questions answered
Quiz passed
Git push
